title: AMS Coding Workflow — How USPS Addresses Are Coded

source.publication: USPS PostalPro — AMS API; USPS RIBBS Address Management Products

chunks.heading: Typical AMS Coding Steps
chunks.text: A typical AMS coding workflow: (1) parse the input address into components (primary number, predirectional, street name, suffix, postdirectional, secondary designator, secondary number, city, state, ZIP); (2) standardize components using Pub 28 rules and USPS reference files; (3) match to the ZIP+4 file to obtain the five-digit ZIP and +4 add-on; (4) apply DPV to confirm the address is a known delivery point; (5) apply LACSLink to update converted rural addresses; (6) apply SuiteLink to append missing secondary information when available; (7) return the coded address with footnotes describing the match outcome.

chunks.heading: Common AMS Return Indicators
chunks.text: AMS-coded results commonly include: a ZIP+4 match flag; a DPV confirmation indicator (Y = confirmed, N = not confirmed, S = confirmed primary but not secondary, D = confirmed primary with missing secondary); footnote codes describing why a secondary was missing or why a CMRA match was detected; and the fully standardized output address. Exact flags vary by AMS API release — consult the current AMS API Developer's Kit user documentation for authoritative codes.

chunks.heading: Data Refresh
chunks.text: USPS AMS data files and the AMS API are updated on a monthly cycle via Electronic Product Fulfillment (EPF). CASS-certified users must use current data to maintain certification; stale data can invalidate mailing discounts.
